title: Angadi — the marketplace
subtitle: a modern reading
intent: Cornering a market is just alignment: hold the right junctions and your rival runs out of room to trade.

start.text: Two trading guilds compete for the crossroads of a market town. Claim three stalls in a connected row and you corner that lane — and force a rival's stall to close.
The ancient alignment game, read as economics: control the junctions that feed two lanes at once, and the market bends toward you.

mill.text: Three stalls in a row — a lane cornered. Market power is alignment: control a line and you set its price.
A row secured from a shared junction. The best positions serve two lanes at once — own those first.
You lock up a lane. Real leverage comes from threatening two at once so a rival can only defend one.

remove.text: A rival stall closes — but you cannot shut one already inside a cornered lane unless all of theirs are. Close the stall that fed their network.
One stall gone. Target the position that gave them options, not merely the easiest to reach.

win.text: The rival guild is down to two stalls and can corner nothing. Positional control, not volume, decided the market.

lose.text: Two stalls left, no lane to corner. The guild that held the key junctions squeezed you out.
